skill.name: command-development
skill.description: Guidance for creating Claude Code slash commands, including frontmatter, arguments, file references, Bash context, organization, plugins, testing, and documentation patterns.
skill.summary: Create well-structured Claude Code slash commands with reusable patterns and safer tool scoping.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell, filesystem, network, and weak-crypto patterns, but review found they are markdown documentation examples rather than bundled executable code. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious behavior was found. The skill should publish with a warning because it teaches Bash-enabled command patterns that users must scope and validate carefully.

medium_findings.title: Shell-Enabled Command Patterns Require Careful Scoping
medium_findings.description: The skill documents inline Bash execution and includes examples with broad Bash permissions and dynamic arguments. This is legitimate for command-development guidance, but users could create unsafe commands if they copy examples without narrowing tool access or validating inputs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations clearly document shell execution patterns, including Bash access and command substitution. Confidence is high for a documentation risk, but not critical because no bundled executable code runs these commands.

medium_findings.title: File Reference Patterns Can Expose Local Content
medium_findings.description: The skill teaches file references, personal command locations, plugin resource paths, and path edge cases. These examples are expected for the topic, but commands built from them can expose local file contents if paths are too broad or attacker-controlled.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence shows file and path handling guidance in documentation. Confidence is moderate-high because the risk depends on how users implement commands from the examples.

low_findings.title: Static Critical Heuristic Is a Documentation False Positive
low_findings.description: The scanner combined examples of shell commands, paths, and network URLs into a critical malicious-behavior heuristic. Review found only markdown guidance and examples, with no executable source files or data exfiltration logic in the bundled skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Repository contents are markdown documentation files, and sampled severe hits are examples or external documentation links. No evidence found of runtime code that executes commands, reads secrets, or sends data over the network.

dangerous_patterns.title: Inline Bash Execution Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Markdown examples use Claude Code inline Bash syntax. This is expected for a command-development skill, but copied commands should use narrow allowed-tools scopes and input validation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The syntax is explicit in the documentation and is a real capability of generated commands. It is not malicious by itself because the skill only documents the pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Bash Permission Example
dangerous_patterns.description: One plugin command example uses broad Bash permissions. This can be risky if copied without reducing access to specific commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The broad permission pattern is visible in a fenced example. The surrounding content is instructional, so the finding is a caution rather than evidence of malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Teams often repeat complex Claude Code workflows by hand. This skill helps create reusable slash commands with clear structure, arguments, tool scopes, and documentation.
actual_capabilities: Explains Claude Code slash command file structure and command locations.
Documents YAML frontmatter fields such as description, model, argument hints, and allowed tools.
Shows how to use dynamic arguments, file references, and inline Bash context in command prompts.
Provides patterns for project, personal, and plugin command organization.
Includes testing, documentation, interactive command, and marketplace guidance for command authors.
Gives examples for review, testing, deployment, documentation, and multi-component workflows.
limitations: The skill is documentation only and does not create command files automatically.
Some examples require Claude Code features and may not work in other assistants.
Bash-enabled command examples require careful tool scoping before production use.
Users must adapt examples to their repository, permissions, and security policy.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Reviews
use_cases.description: Create shared review, testing, and documentation commands that give Claude consistent instructions across a repository.

use_cases.title: Build Plugin Command Sets
use_cases.description: Design plugin commands that reference plugin resources, scripts, agents, and skills in a portable structure.

use_cases.title: Document Command Behavior
use_cases.description: Write command descriptions, argument hints, usage examples, and test checklists for maintainable command libraries.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Claude Code slash command for reviewing changed files. Include the file path, frontmatter, argument hint, and the command prompt text.

prompt_templates.title: Add Safe Arguments
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a slash command that accepts a pull request number and priority. Include validation guidance and explain how each argument is used.

prompt_templates.title: Scope Tool Access
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a slash command that gathers git status before analysis. Use the narrowest allowed-tools setting and explain why the scope is safe.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Plugin Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a plugin slash command workflow that uses a plugin script, a template file, and a specialist agent. Include organization, validation, and testing notes.

output_examples.input: User asks for a command that reviews a source file.
output_examples.output: The skill produces a command plan with a clear description, an argument hint for the file path, a file reference pattern, and review criteria.

output_examples.input: User asks how to add Bash context to a command.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends a narrow allowed-tools setting, shows where inline command output belongs, and warns against broad shell permissions.

output_examples.input: User asks for plugin command organization.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines command directories, namespace behavior, resource references, and testing steps for a plugin command suite.

best_practices: Use narrow allowed-tools entries and avoid broad Bash access unless there is a clear reason.
Document arguments with argument-hint and validate required inputs inside the command prompt.
Keep each command focused on one workflow and provide examples for expected usage.
anti_patterns: Do not copy Bash(*) examples into production commands without narrowing command access.
Do not pass untrusted arguments directly into shell commands without validation.
Do not store credentials, tokens, or private environment details inside command files.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation skill that explains how to write Claude Code slash commands.

faq.question: Can it help with command frontmatter?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains fields such as description, argument-hint, model, allowed-tools, and disable-model-invocation.

faq.question: Does it support plugin commands?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers plugin command folders, plugin resource references, and multi-component workflows.

faq.question: Are Bash examples safe to use directly?
faq.answer: They need review before use. Restrict allowed tools and validate any user-supplied arguments.

faq.question: Can it create commands for non-Claude tools?
faq.answer: The guidance is focused on Claude Code slash commands. Other tools may need different syntax.

faq.question: What should I provide when asking for a command?
faq.answer: Provide the workflow goal, expected arguments, needed files, allowed tools, and any team security constraints.
